chunk_summary: Nutrition-first management of growth faltering after six months focuses on optimizing feeding practices, energy density, and addressing medical contributors before extensive investigations, using WHO growth standards for monitoring.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of meal frequency, energy density, and milk displacement in growth faltering after six months.
Learn how to optimize feeding practices, including age-appropriate meal/snack targets and energy-dense food additions.
Identify medical contributors to growth faltering and recognize signs of severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Utilize WHO growth standards to monitor weight trends and growth velocity.
key_facts: Recommended meal/snack targets: 6–8 months: 2–3 meals; 9–11 months: 3 meals + snacks; 12–24 months: 3 meals + 2 snacks.
Limit cow’s milk intake to <500 mL/day and eliminate juice/sugary drinks.
Enhance energy density by adding ghee/oil, peanut powder, or dairy to staples.
Screen for anemia, recurrent infections, chronic diarrhea, cow’s milk protein allergy, tuberculosis exposure, and congenital heart disease if dietary optimization fails.
Urgently evaluate for severe wasting, nutritional edema, or feeding refusal with lethargy, as these suggest SAM or systemic illness requiring facility-based care.
reference_quote: Plot weight-for-age and weight-for-length using WHO growth standards and look for growth velocity; a single low percentile is less concerning than downward crossing.
document_key_topics: Age-appropriate feeding practices and energy density
Screening for medical contributors to poor growth
Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) and systemic illness
